people.notes: The 1496 PCC will/index evidence now supplies a direct identity bridge: the same probate index entry for Richard Conggrave, gentleman, associates Loughton, Essex and Penkeryche/Penkridge, Staffordshire, and TNA D1057 independently says the Richard Congreve who died in 1496 lived at Laughton/Loughton while cataloguing the Congreve and Stretton family papers. The former separate Stretton pedigree Richard has therefore been collapsed into this record. The 1496 will directly names Richard's wife Johane, son John Congrave, and daughter Agnes Congrave. The 1471 West Tilbury and 1489 Latton fines both put Richard Congreve in Essex land settlements with Harpur/Harpur-circle parties, and the Latton limitation to the heirs of Richard Congreve is stronger than a trustee-only mention. The 1492 Harpour will discussion separately strengthens the Essex-Harpur-Congreve bridge by naming Christopher as son of Richard Congreve and Anne, sister of Richard Harper; Christopher remains a cautious candidate child because the original Harpour will has not been inspected and Anne/Johane still needs reconciliation. The later visitation pedigree details are useful for the Stretton frame and Ralfe link, but should still be checked against original deeds and the complete PCC will before treating every pedigree detail as settled.

people.notes: Primary display form is now Reginald Conigrave. Source spellings include Reginald Conygrave and Reynold Conygrave, which are preserved as alternate names; a Chancery catalogue also gives alias Conye. The London inquisition-post-mortem abstract quotes Reginald's will dated 7 May 1550 and names sons George, Thomas, Edward, Mark, Robert, William, and Edmund, but no inspected source names Reginald's father. George, Thomas, Edward, Robert, William, and Edmund now have person records from the will/IPM abstract alone. Mark Conigrave of Oxford/Stoke Albany remains the current in-database candidate for the named son Mark, but the identity is not proven. The 1548 freemen-register reference to Joan as executrix conflicts with the 1550 IPM death date if read literally, so it should be checked against the damaged manuscript context before being used for death chronology.

father.notes: No source names Reginald's father. John Congrave, son of Richard Congrave of Loughton, is now the sharper candidate because Richard's 1496 will proves a son John and Reginald's 1530s-1550 London/Bedfordshire career sits in the plausible next generation. Richard Congrave/Congreve of Loughton/Laughton remains a broader candidate bridge through the 1471-1496 Essex-Harpur-Congreve cluster, but neither link is proven.

people.notes: Added explicitly as requested. This is a strong lived record, not a birth/death record. The likely movement path is now London family/probate context in 1550, Oxford university attestation in 1556-1557, then Stoke Albany rectory from 1561 to 1614. The Oxford University register records Mark Conigrave or Cunygrave in the 1556-1557 B.A. sequence, and Bardsley's surname note independently pointed to the same register citation. The London inquisition-post-mortem abstract for Reginald Conygrave's 1550 will names a son Mark, making Reginald a strong candidate father, but the identity is not yet proven. No source-backed birth or baptism has been found; Stoke Albany's surviving parish register begins in 1575, too late for a man already in Oxford by 1556 and rector by 1561. Forebears' Bardsley extract separately identifies Mark Conigrave or Cunygrave in the 1556 Register of the University of Oxford.
